Correction Note

In The International Journal of Psychoanalysis Volume 93 Issue 6, A Film Essay, An entire life in one glance: a psychoanalytic reading of Revolutionary Road by Paola Golinelli and Nicolino Rossi. Unfortunately it failed to mention that it was translated into English by Gina Atkinson, who did a superb job. Paola Golinelli and Nicolino Rossi would like to acknowledge their thanks to her.

[[RunningHead]]

Correction Note

Minding The Markets: An Emotional Finance View of Financial Instability by David Tuckett reviewed by Stephen M. Sonnenberg. DOI: 10.1111/j.1745-8315.2012.00665.x

Some amendments have been made to this book review, following publication in The International Journal of Psychoanalysis, Volume 94, Issue 1. The changes occur on page 189, last paragraph. A link was not included and another was placed in the wrong position.

The corrected text should read:

In a search for more information I went to the website Tuckett refers to in the book, as a place where more interview data can be found [http://www.minding-the-markets.com]. Unfortunately, on many repeated attempts I could not connect to the site. A useful site I did find, the Minding the Markets website [http://www.palgrave.com/finance/mindingthemarkets/], did bring me to some good interview data and information about the study.

The text should not read:

In a search for more information I went to the website Tuckett refers to in the book, as a place where more interview data can be found [http://www.palgrave.com/finance/mindingthemarkets/]. Unfortunately, on many repeated attempts I could not connect to the site. A useful site I did find, the Minding the Markets website, did bring me to some good interview data and information about the study.

We apologize for this error and regret any inconvenience caused.

619

‘The exodus from Egypt remains our point of departure’: The Covert Tradition in Sigmund Freud's Moses and Monotheism by Yigal Blumenberg2Brandes & Apsel, Frankfurt/M, 2012; 428 pp; 39,80€

Wolfgang Hegener

Wolfgang Hegener

An der Stammbahn 67, DE-14532, Kleinmachnow, Germany E-mail: w.hegener@t-online.de

Wolfgang Hegener  

A number of years ago Harold Bloom (1990) formulated the thesis that the supreme intellectual accomplishments of modern Judaism were due primarily to three authors: Franz Kafka, Gershom Scholem and Sigmund Freud. This claim is impressively corroborated and fleshed out by the book under discussion here using meticulous (textual) analyses. The book centres around Freud's late work and legacy, Moses and Monotheism [original German title: Der Mann Moses und die monotheistische Religion], which is not only a treatise on cultural theory but also carries within it the legacy of the whole of Freudian psychoanalysis. Although Freud's Moses

[[RunningHead]]

A clínica psicanalítica das psicopatologias contemporâneas [The Psychoanalytic Clinic of Contemporary Psychopathologies]; by Gley P. Costa et al.2; Artmed, Porto Alegre, 2010; 272 pp

Rogério Souza

Rogério Souza

Rua Morgado de Mateus 651, 04015-051, São Paulo, SP, Brazil
E-mail: coelr0307@terra.com.br

Rogério Souza  

It is extremely gratifying to come across a text in the field of psychoanalysis which illuminates but at the same time provokes debate; only a writer of some considerable skill is able to achieve this. However, the description is undoubtedly true of Gley Silva de Pacheco Costa, a psychoanalyst of deep and wide-ranging clinical experience and gifted teacher, and his work in producing this book. Costa's writing is gaining in stature in Brazilian, and even international, psychoanalysis because he tackles areas of current concern, as he does in this book, but he is also known for the outstanding lucidity and erudition of his accounts of clinical investigations. Several chapters of this book are written with colleagues, demonstrating the spirit of collaboration and willingness to recognize contributions from fellow academics that characterize Costa's analytic stance and dedication to psychoanalysis. Highly regarded by many of his psychoanalyst colleagues including notable practitioners, this author and his work deserve to be more widely appreciated and recognized. This review is intended to go some way towards achieving this.

Has anything about psychoanalysis altered as it has been accompanying the socio-cultural change we have been going through? Do we have a proper grip on the causes of the illnesses peculiar to human existence in our present time, based on the psychopathological expressions we see in our patients in our everyday clinical activity?

2Translated by Suzanne Jones.1262

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen  

En toute chose, nous ne pouvons être jugés que par nos pairs.1

Balzac, 1830

….the impulse of curiosity on which all learning depends…

Bion, 1959

Since 1920, following its foundation by Ernest Jones under the direction of Sigmund Freud, the International Journal of Psychoanalysis (incorporating the International Review of Psychoanalysis) has been the leading place for psychoanalysts to read and develop ideas across the globe.

It will continue unchanged in this form. Now, however, and with pleasure I announce a new and important development enabled by modern technology. As from January 2014, anyone who logs on to http://www.ijp-open.org will be able to participate directly and in real time in creating a new publication - to be called IJP Open.

IJP Open will be the launch pad leading to the selection, development and final publication of papers in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis.

For the first time in the history of psychoanalytic journals, we are providing readers with the opportunity to view the papers submitted to the International Journal of Psychoanalysis before peer review, and the opportunity to participate in a peer discussion of those papers, engaging with and developing critical thought.

This means that from January authors submitting to the International Journal of Psychoanalysis will have their work read by their colleagues almost immediately and will therefore have the opportunity to rapidly engage with a large community of peers and benefit from their ideas. The authors will also have a chance to respond. Authors will be able to have their work read in the language of submission (English, Spanish, French, Portuguese, Italian or German), creating a thoroughly international platform for the dissemination and advancement of psychoanalytic ideas.

For the first time readers will also have access to the reports written by the IJP reviewers, demystifying the peer review process.

IJP Open is a completely new type of publication in psychoanalysis. It aims to involve a large clinical, theoretical and research community in publishing ideas, giving fast access to the latest psychoanalytic thinking, and enabling participation in an innovative and open peer review process. Authors will have their work read and known very quickly.

responseSet.title: On the Rorschach Protocol
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Rorschach Protocol

Justine Woods

Justine Woods

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA, England E-mail: jmwoodspsych@live.co.uk

Justine Woods  

Introduction

The Rorschach is a test that requires a careful analysis of the ‘location’ of the responses, as provided by the subject, but this information unfortunately was not available for this protocol. In addition, the ‘Inquiry’, which allows us to understand the thinking and other processes behind the response, was also absent from this protocol. This means that the interpretation of the protocol which follows is incomplete and can only be considered as a set of hypotheses about the personality functioning of this man.

Interpretation of the Scoring and Responses

This man appeared to engage reasonably well with the Rorschach where he was forthcoming in providing responses, in fact providing more responses than the average individual. He is someone who appeared able to recognize the ‘right’ or ‘correct’ way to do things and so was likely to have acted in a ‘conventional’ manner in his day-to-day life when he was convinced that this would be in his best interests, but nevertheless appearing to retain a preference for doing things his own way.

The Rorschach findings suggest that this man had a tendency to think about his experiences in a rather inflexible manner, where he was likely to stick to his convictions and resist changing his beliefs, even in the light of new information. Usually, an individual such as this is less likely to engage in therapy or psychoanalysis over the medium to longer term. However, it is apparent from the Rorschach findings that this man was deeply troubled and conflicted, which may explain why he sought psychotherapeutic help.

From the very first response (“like a bird without head”), a theme emerges which pervades the entire protocol, which suggested that possibly he feels that he is losing his mind, that he cannot think, function properly, or where there is something that he cannot bear to think or to let himself know about. For example, for his next response, he says: “Could also be a woman - without head”. For the next card, his second response for this card includes the following, “everywhere the heads are missing”, and so on. Indeed, through his Rorschach responses he enables us to gain access to his inner world, to witness the damage, destruction and fragmentation.

Rorschach Protocol

Copy Rorschach Test, 8.50 - 9.25
I. 1. Like a bird - an eagle without head (W) - W F/- A
2. Could also be a woman - without head (middle figure) - torse D F/- Hd
3. Could also be a bat V F/- A P
II. 1. Battle between two men or bears, who push against each other with knees and hands D M(/-) A/H P
2. Could be a vagina (S)(?) the hole (Back to 1) everywhere the heads are missing (does not sea red; men = black) DS F- Sex
3. This could be blood (red middle) D C(/-) Blood
4. Heads looking outward, with tiasas, nose missing (lateral, not belonging to 1) — heads of Assyrians Dd F - Hd O
III. 1. Two boys (waiters), as if they were pulling, holding something - bag? W M(/-) H P
2. Skeleton: pelvis (red middle) D F- Anat. [/-?]
3. Could be a rooster (red lateral), only the tall a bit different D F/- A
IV. 1. Like one sitting on a post, the head bent, one does not see it (WM) W M(/-) H
V. 1. Looks like asflying bat - could also be a butterfly — it looks to the right W F/- A P
VI. 1. Hide as one sees it at a tanner's (leaf) D F/- A P
2. Some insect (top), not definable D F/- A
3. Scrotum (upper middle) Dd F/- Sex
VII. 1. Coralgrowth (W) [?] jagged W F- Corals
2. Like anus - fish, lies - man or woman -thigh (upper third, left and right) with vagina and anus D F- Sex/Anal
3. Bird, standing, penguin, raising its head (upper third, towards left) (m) D F/- A
4. Diabolicalface (middle third), goggle-eyes with horns D F/- Hd P
VIII. Ah, colours!
1. Animals - in the stapes - mole or martin (side figures) D F/- A P
2. Which are climbing the root of the tree (gray) D F/- P1.
3. Like two pillows (blue left and right) [?] the form - as if plastic, standing out, the edge jagged D F(fb)(+) Obj.
4. Could be a butterfly die Form(?) die Form - who D F/- A
5. is hanging on a cord (blue extension lower middle). (Why coloured? Significance of colour?) Dd F/- Obj.
IX. 1. Could be horns of stag (fork) and the back (rest of brown) D F/- Ad
2. Head of sheep (green lateral) - the body chopped off like a pipe (dark green) D F/- Ad
3. Stones (red lateral, right and left) D F- Stones
4. Thigh and vagina (red middle) D F- Sex
5. Part of violin (lower half of S) DS F- Obj.
X. 1. Little men, like devils, with horns and tail, or D F/- H
2. Littlebirds, chicks (gray middle) D F- A
3. Crabs (blue lateral) D F/- A P
4. Caterpillars (lower half of red, left and right) D F- A
5. Two men (red left and right - later with medial gray), who touch with their D M/- H

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Fiancée Letters: “Be mine as I envision you” [“Sei mein, wie ich mir's denke. Die Brautbriefe”], Vol. 1: June 1882-July 1883 by Sigmund Freud, Martha Bernays edited by Gerhard Fichtner, Ilse Grubrich-Simitis and Albrecht HirschmüllerFischer, Frankfurt am Main, 2011; 628 pp; €48

Hannsjörg von Freytag-Loringhoven

Hannsjörg von Freytag-Loringhoven

Engelfriedshalde 65, D-72076 Tübingen, Germany
E-mail: hjfrey@supra-net.net

Hannsjörg von Freytag-Loringhoven  

Curiosity for The Fiancée Letters was piqued decades ago by Ernest Jones in his biography of Freud and by Freud's son, Ernst. The first volume of the complete set of five (1882-1886) has just now been published in German. It was presented at the opening of the 47th International Psychoanalytic Association Congress in Mexico City, in honour of the 100th Anniversary of the founding of the IPA, by one of the editors: psychoanalyst and Freud researcher, Ilse Grubrich-Simitis. It is her view that The Fiancée Letters describe the progress of a man who initially presented himself in the role of a radical paternal authority, but who then learned that his fiancée would accept this stance only on the condition that he accepted her as his peer. This hypothesis may provide some understanding of why Martha, even as his beloved, repeatedly referred to him by his doctor title, as if in jest. In actuality, this was not an act of submission! By addressing him in this manner, she was meeting the autonomy afforded him by his high academic standing with the equality of her own femininity. By doing so, she was unaware that she was also setting forth a basic tenet of later psychoanalysis.

It was precisely in this way that psychoanalysis differentiated itself from the other treatment methods of the day. This principle of equality which Martha Bernays implicitly introduced quite simply conveyed the understanding that an authentic human relationship can only come into being if the more powerful figure forgoes some of the use of his power. Sigmund Freud developed this principle further by explicitly introducing the rule of abstinence: every encounter is set up from the start for him to remain himself. Even in the process of analysis when everything imaginable might develop in his internal life, for example, demands, compulsions, even maturity. One can observe the various psychic states in the quotes that preface each volume. There is something monistic, single-minded in Freud's demand: “Be mine, as I imagine you”; perhaps a triangulated aspect in Freud's declaration: “To have you just as you are” (p. 37). As he gradually let this necessary duality deepen within him, Freud was, “after struggle and agitation” (p. 37) in a position to accept Martha Bernays's autonomy. Excited by an endearing letter from her, he wrote back on 4 July 1883: “I am happy that you can write like this after I offended you so … I was very worried, anticipating your reply” (p. 496). He now entered the ‘depressive position’, later discovered and coined as a psychoanalytic term by Melanie Klein. Martha Bernays claimed her equality a year earlier on 12 July 1882 with the words: “I assure you of my full, exclusive, intimate love, but also wish that you not disturb my affectionate, friendly sentiments for my old friend” (p. 183). Even in this early interaction, it was always Martha who came to the rescue of the situation without, however, relinquishing her own autonomy.

responseSet.title: On: Trying to Strengthen the Psychoanalytic Theory and Discipline
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Trying to Strengthen the Psychoanalytic Theory and Discipline

René Epstein

René Epstein

Juncal 2514, CP 1425 City of Buenos Aires, Argentina
E-mail: reneps@sion.com; epstein.rene@gmail.com

René Epstein  

Dear Editors,

In IJP 93(2) R. S. Wallerstein (2012) addresses the insularity of psychoanalysis, suggesting ways in which psychoanalysis might secure a stronger position within the university where it has the potential to intersect with other disciplines. However, it is not clear whether psychoanalysis has been structured as a discipline at all (as considered by Freud). Other recent articles in the IJP have looked at why psychoanalysis fails to integrate with ‘outside’ fields. Namer (2011) suggests that psychoanalytical groups are consolidated via their ‘search for an object’, related to professional practice, and influencing theory. One of the theoretical effects of the group ‘object choice’ has been to delete the differences between psychoanalysis and psychodynamic psychotherapy. Such groups tend to uphold ‘postmodern’ ideas, which oppose academic formalizations of knowledge, but at the same time they sustain a definitive monistic truth, like the one they criticize, focusing on individualistic ways of thinking which increases the failure of psychoanalysis to intersect with ‘outside’ disciplines.

I would like to add that the development of the professionalization of psychoanalysis has created further problems for its integration with the ‘outside’. It has encouraged simplified classifications of psychoanalysis as a natural science, a humanistic science, or a kind of hermeneutics (as well as fostering naïve views of science based on empirical research or confrontations between quantitative and qualitative research). In endorsing scientific-philosophical pluralism, the professionalization of psychoanalysis is also dangerously opposed to a methodology of knowledge.

Influenced by Bhaskarian thought (Epstein, 2010), my central argument is that every discipline should be understood to have its own epistemology and methodology, a singular combination determined by the ontology of its object and the practical circumstances in which knowledge is applied, thus including definitions of what is ‘interscientific’. And the singularity of each discipline must be taken into account when we try to develop its ‘interdisciplinary’ fields. Psychoanalysis, as a profession, and a liberal one, has its own ingrained ideology, an unconscious ‘socio-political’ position that isn't easily integrated within the academic realm. The epistemological problem to be solved is complex and can be expressed, for instance, in the contrast among quite different gnoseologic levels of a practice that alternate between: (1) the ‘general’ theory of each individual's psyche; (2) the general theory of the psyche, as well as the idiosyncratic complexity of each therapeutic relationship and the technical-methodological considerations of a therapy and its theory, diverse levels (and the in-between) hardly able to be uni vocally encoded.

responseSet.title: On: Reply to Levine
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Reply to Levine

Jay Greenberg

Jay Greenberg

275 Central Park West, Apt 1BB, New York, New York, 10024, United States
Phone: 212 595-5927
E-mail: jaygreenberg275@aol.com

Jay Greenberg  

Dear Editor,

I appreciate Howard Levine's close reading of my commentary on Jose Bleger's groundbreaking paper.

Clearly, Levine and I have different interpretations of Bleger's intent: where I described his paper as “more manifesto than reasoned argument” he sees a less drastic movement away from “received” versions of Freudian theory and practice. To put it another way, Levine sees the paper as additive to the Freudian tradition while I read it as proposing a radical break from that tradition.

Naturally, I think my reading stays closer to Bleger's project than does Levine's. Looking at Bleger's vision of the structure of psychoanalytic theory I take very seriously his statement that: “Dynamics are a representation or model of dramatics but not its cause” (Bleger, 2012, p. 996). And I take very seriously his application of this to clinical process: “psychoanalytic technique and practice … operate entirely in dramatics” (p. 996, my italics). This suggests to me that Bleger is not simply adding a new way of thinking that will apply to some patients at some times while preserving more established ways of thinking and working with other patients at other times. Rather, in my view, he is sweeping out one model and ushering in another.

I draw further support for this from Bleger's approach to the etiological role of sexuality. He writes: “Sexuality is one of the vicissitudes of a Gestalt in which we now give priority to psychotic anxieties” (p. 1000, my italics). To my eye this goes a long way toward undermining the diagnostic distinctions that shape Levine's “two-track” model.

573

The Intimate Room: Theory and Technique of the Analytic Field by Giuseppe Civitarese (Philip Slotkin, translator) Routledge, London, 2010, the New Library of Psychoanalysis Series; 240 pp; £23.99 paperback

James Rose

James Rose

Brandon Centre
26, Prince of Wales Rd.
London
United Kingdom
NW5 6LG
E-mail: james.s.rose@blueyonder.co.uk

James Rose  

Subtitled as Theory and Technique of the Analytic Field, I feel that it must be said at the outset that this is not a book for beginners. It seeks to offer a perspective on the psychoanalytic process from the point of view of modern literary theory and post-modernist philosophical analysis. The reader most likely to be fully engaged by this text is one who is practising or has practised psycho-analysis in the working sense. Without this experience and all its rigours, I found it hard to see how a reader would be able to engage with the text in a meaningful way. In itself, this is no criticism but I say it because I think readers should be ready to examine their professional experience and to have taken a position, implicitly or explicitly, on the various issues that it raises. Thus, while the book might seem to read like a statement, I think the reader may find it more fruitful to use it to engage in a debate with themselves and the author about what they do and why they do it their way.

Indeed, because the author, Guiseppe Civitarese, seeks to examine the work of psychoanalysis from points of view that were unfamiliar to me, I found that reading the book was an experience not unlike an analysis. As examples of the issues raised, he explores the nature of psychic reality, the nature and function of the setting, the nature of transference and the nature and function of memory.

201

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen

Private Pratice, 7a Ellerdale Road, London NW3 6BA, UK
E-mail: dana.birksted-breen@dib.me.uk

Dana Birksted-Breen  

Avant l'oeuvre, oeuvre d'art, oeuvre d'écriture, oeuvre de parole,

il n'y a pas d'artiste, ni d'écrivain, ni

de sujet parlant, puisque c'est

la production qui produit le producteur,

le faisant naǐtre ou apparaǐtre en le prouvant.1

(Maurice Blanchot, 1983, pp. 85-6)

A year into the establishment of the new Editorial Board, the International Journal welcomes the opportunity to look back at developments over the last year and forward to new possibilities, as well as reflect on the defining values and outlook of the Journal.

A core concern of the International Journal is the creative space between tradition and change, where development stems from psychoanalytic foundations in a dialectical movement of returning to the past and looking forward. Probing the limits of psychoanalysis, as editor I try to stay attentive to the questions - what is the psychoanalytical? Where are its parameters? When does it become so changed that it is no longer psychoanalysis? These are not simple questions and need constant discussion if our profession is to retain its solid foundation, providing a frame for creativity. Innovation emerges from previously established parameters and resides in specific, sometimes subtle, shifts in thought. Speaking of the developments that take place in the consulting room, but also in theoretical thought, Jean Laplanche identifies a dialectic between “centering” and “recentering” (Laplanche, 1999, pp. 2, 67), where critique remains internal to the economy of Freudian thought and metapsychological concepts, but also other to it, revising rather than rejecting the past.

In the face of opposition to the foundation and clinical setting of psychoanalysis both from within and outside the field, how we define psychoanalysis requires ongoing discussion and delineation of concepts. In fact, this was a concern of the International Journal from its very inception; in his first Editorial, Ernest Jones spoke of “two forms of defence [against psychoanalysis], open opposition and what has been well called ‘wild Psycho-Analysis’”, where the latter “may assume subtle forms also amongst those having a nearer acquaintance with the subject” (1920, p. 4). Almost a century on and the field has developed considerably in significant ways, but Jones's words remain pertinent, reminding us how far we have come but also that we should be alert to attempts to dissipate our foundations and stay vigilant to the question - what is psychoanalysis?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response by Charles Hanly

Charles Hanly

Charles Hanly

106 Elm Ave., Toronto, Ontario, Canada M4W 1P2 - charlesmhanly@gmail.com

Charles Hanly  

The recent series of papers on Winnicott's technical innovations (Abram, 2012; Blass, 2012; Bonaminio, 2012; Eigen, 2012) provides us with interesting reflections on Winnicott's contribution to adult analysis.2 It raises the question of whether the importance Winnicott attaches to the analyst's role of providing good enough parenting is consistent with a traditional or classical view of psychoanalysis. If Freud and Klein affirm the primacy of interpretation of the dynamic psychic unconscious in the analytic cure, as I believe they do (Hanly, 1994), then the answer would have to be that Winnicott's view is not consistent with a traditional view of psychoanalysis. In what follows I will explain my reasoning.

Although Grunbaum (1984) exaggerated Freud's reliance on the tally argument, there is no question that Freud considered the sound interpretations of what is actively unconscious in the patient in the analysis as the primary and indispensable instruments of therapeutic efficacy. Freud (1914, 1917, 1937a, 1940) did not disregard the fact that psychoanalysis is a human relation, but he was primarily concerned with considerations about the possibility of analytic collaboration, rather than with advocating building a relationship in which the analyst is able, by means of empathy or emotional responsiveness, to provide the patient with what life has deprived him/her (except in so far as the analysis can enable him/her to mourn its loss and to find substitute satisfactions in present life). Similarly, Freud (1914) realized that the therapeutic results he sought could not be achieved simply by the analyst putting conflicts, unconscious wishes and symptoms into words. If the necessary maturation of instinctual organizations were to take place, then these words had to be found by the patient in the transforming affect of the work of association during analysis. In this painful struggle, Freud consistently sees the analyst's role as attending to the patient's process of repetition and working through, as the patient is in search of instinct maturation while resisting the work required by it.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: From Basle to Milan to Zurich: On Psychoanalytic Training

Pier Galli MD

Pier Galli

www.psicoterapiaescienzeumane.it
Via Garibaldi 3, 40124
Bologna, Italy
E-mail: pierfrancescogalli@libero.it

Pier Galli  

Dear Editor,

D. Bürgin's (2012) Letter from Basle contains information on the history of the ‘Psychoanalytic Seminar Zurich’ (PSZ) - namely on the reason why it became independent from the Swiss Society of Psychoanalysis - that needs to be corrected. But first I have to say few words on my involvement with the PSZ, and in general on my cultural background and professional career both in Italy and in Switzerland. Some biographical information on my part is also important because, as I will explain, I was personally involved with a conflict between the Swiss Society of Psychoanalysis and both the German (DPV) and Vienna (WPV) psychoanalytic associations. In this way I would like to complement Dr Bürgin's letter with some information so that the readers of this Journal can have a more complete picture of the history of psychoanalytic movement in these countries in the 1960s-70s. At the same time I would like to contribute to the long-standing discussion on controversial aspects of psychoanalytic training.

After medical school in Italy I was trained both in general and experimental psychology in Milan, and in 1957 I moved to Basle to work with Gaetano Benedetti (mentioned by Dr Bürgin) on his pioneering research in the psychotherapy of psychotic patients. We formed a group of colleagues with heterogeneous orientations. Members of this group were classical analysts, phenomenologists and dasein-analysts, Jungians, and also some pupils of Peter Szondi. I can mention Ludwig Binswanger Jr, Norman Elrod who had a Jungian orientation, Raymond Battegay who would later become an important researcher on group therapy, Leo Wurmser who would move to the US, Allan Johansson and Ajno Kärkäinen-Kilta from Finland; other Finnish analysts, in close collaboration with Benedetti, were Kauko Kaila and Martti Siirala, who greatly contributed to the study of family therapy in Zurich. From Germany, Walter Bräutigam was working with us, before going to the University of Heidelberg where he would hold the Chair of Psychiatry, and so on.

responseSet.title: Discussion of Hermann Argelander's paper: ‘The scenic function of the ego and its role in symptom and character formation’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of Hermann Argelander's paper: ‘The scenic function of the ego and its role in symptom and character formation’

Leon Balter

Leon Balter

544 East 86th Street, New York, NY 10028, USA - leonbalter@aol.com

Leon Balter  

I would like to express my appreciation to the International Journal of Psychoanalysis for organizing a forum where papers written by analysts from one part of the international psychoanalytic community are discussed by analysts from other parts. I had suspected that such discussions would manifest a phenomenon analogous to what Darwin (1859, Chapters 11, 12) described as the origin of new species promoted by geographical isolation. Indeed, there does appear to be the formation of more or less geographically isolated psychoanalytic communities, all adhering to and deriving from the works of Sigmund Freud, but nevertheless actively evolving their own distinct terminological, theoretical and technical particularities. They are influenced to widely varying degrees by the local intellectual, social or national ambience and/or the personal authority of one or more local psychoanalytic teacher(s). (Ludwik Fleck (1979), the sociologist of science, would term these psychoanalytic communities ‘thought collectives’.) This intellectual isolation in our profession sometimes obtains to such a degree that differences in definitions, standards of evidence, and conceptions of psychoanalytic treatment make communication among separated psychoanalytic communities very difficult, if not impossible.

This then was my anxious anticipation about discussing a recently-translated 1970 paper by Dr. Hermann Argelander who was then at the Sigmund Freud Institute in Frankfurt. My own very local psychoanalytic ‘ecology’ pivots around American ego psychology, and particularly that of the New York Psychoanalytic Institute and Society with its own specific accents and preoccupations. My anxieties were markedly ameliorated when I realized that Argelander happily based most of his argument on many luminaries of American ego psychology. I presume this is what Werner Bohleber means, in his Introduction to Argelander's paper, where he states: “However, Argelander's thinking always preserved its links to ego psychology.” But also, of greatest interest to me, Bohleber pointed to the great influence of Critical Theory and other German philosophical tendencies on postwar German psychoanalysis, the resulting predominantly hermeneutic orientation, and Argelander's leadership in that trend. As Bohleber notes, this is an intellectual tendency that de-emphasizes psychoanalysis as a natural science. By contrast, psychoanalysis as a natural science is an intensely cathected intellectual orientation of American ego psychology. It is the historical product of American psychoanalysis's long-standing close relation with psychiatry and medicine in general.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Nouveaux Développements en Psychanalyse: Autour de la pensée de Michel de M'Uzan edited by Clarisse Baruch EDK, Paris, 2011; 158 pp; £ 18

Ellen Sparer

Ellen Sparer

57 rue de Turbigo, 75003 Paris, France
E-mail: easparer@gmail.com

Ellen Sparer  

In March 2009, a tribute to Michel de M'Uzan, pillar of French psychoanalysis for more than 50 years, was organized by a research group at the University of Paris-Ouest-Nanterre. At the initiative of Professor Emeritus C. Baruch, the research group directed by Professor D. Cupa structured the conference as a dialogue between Michel de M'Uzan and several colleagues. Nouveaux Développements en Psychanalyse [Recent Developments in Psychoanalysis] brings together in a single volume the interventions of the conference, all of which were responses to M. de M'Uzan's opening intervention: ‘Reconsiderations and New Developments in Psychoanalysis’.

With elegance and concision, Michel de M'Uzan presents and then discusses, with several eminent colleagues, his current thinking, ‘reconsiderations’ regarding theory, clinical work and technique in psychoanalysis today.

Three factors provide the basis for Michel de M'Uzan's decision to reconsider psychoanalysis: theory, clinical work and technique. He opens his essay with a discussion of three theoretical propositions: the notion of instinctual movement [pulsion], and in particular the death instinct; Freud's abandonment of his ‘neurotica’ in 1897, that is, the theory of seduction; and finally, with regard to structural theory, which is called in French the second topographical theory, M. de M'Uzan ‘reconsiders’ Freud's second instinctual theory.

A firm adherent of the topographical theory, M. de M'Uzan argues that, if one is rigorous metapsychologically, an instinctual movement can only be psychosexual, given that it has a source, a pressure, an aim and an object. It would follow then that, strictly speaking, there is no self-preservation instinct. In Freud's first instinctual theory, sexual instincts attach themselves to vital “functions serving the purpose of self-preservation” (Freud, SE 7, p. 182) before proceeding towards auto-eroticism. De M'Uzan argues that these “vital-identity” functions belong to an essentially genetic program from which the (sexual) instinctual movement emerges.

responseSet.title: Like a Pebble in Your Shoe: A Psychoanalytical Reading of Lars von Trier's Breaking the Waves and Antichrist
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Like a Pebble in Your Shoe: A Psychoanalytical Reading of Lars von Trier's Breaking the Waves and Antichrist

Judy Gammelgaard

Judy Gammelgaard

Gassehaven 69, 2840 Holte, Denmark - judy.gammelgaard@psy.ku.dk

Judy Gammelgaard  

Introduction

Lars von Trier has never made a secret of the fact that his films are intended to provoke and has succeeded to the degree that he has become the enfant terrible of contemporary cinema. Indeed, his films have evoked strong ambivalent feelings in most spectators, not least Antichrist, the most reviewed and discussed film in the year it appeared. Critics and commentators alike have wondered how to evaluate the purpose, ethics and aesthetic quality of this movie; one critic called it “a masterpiece disguised as junk” (Skotte, 2009).

Masterpiece or junk? The extremity of these designations ought to make us wonder why von Trier's films tend to receive such contradictory responses. Precisely what is it that inspires the conflicting reception and judgements?

The title of this paper is taken from von Trier (Drouzy, 1991) whose films provoke the incessant and uncomfortable feeling of having a pebble in one's shoe. His films provoke us to ask us whether we like or dislike them, or whether we like some of them while at the same time are left confused, bewildered, or even offended. The irritation caused by a pebble, however, is a somewhat inadequate image to describe the audiences' immediate reactions to the cruelty, corporal mutilation and depression featured in von Trier's films.

Commentators and critics have pointed to trauma as the central theme in von Trier films, played out at the narrative as well as at the formal and effective level (Bainbridge, 2004a, b, 2007; Badley, 2010). While this term - so familiar to psychoanalysts - does indeed capture the essence of von Trier's cinematic universe, what we meet with cannot be understood within the framework of the trauma models we know from classical psychoanalytic theory, that is, the model of traumatic neuroses or Nachträglichkeit. The traumatic effect seems rather to stem from images devoid of meaning and which reach the spectator as an excess of unbound perception.

The aim of this paper is to explore how we may understand - from a psychoanalytic perspective - the traumatic moment as the distinctive mark of von Trier's filmmaking. I have chosen Breaking the Waves (1996)

[[RunningHead]]

Minding the Markets: An Emotional Finance View of Financial Instability by David TuckettPalgrave Macmillan, Basingstoke, Hants, 2011; 260 pp; £26.00

Stephen Sonnenberg

Stephen Sonnenberg

1600 West 38th Street, Suite 403, Austin,
Texas 78731, USA
E-mail: ssonnenberg@utexas.edu

Stephen Sonnenberg  

Most readers of this book review know David Tuckett as the former Editor in Chief of this Journal. But, in Minding The Markets, Professor Tuckett tells us he is trained in economics and sociology as well as psychoanalysis. As one reads on, Tuckett makes strategic references to Neil Smelser and Talcott Parsons, two true giants in the recent history of the social sciences. Smelser, in fact, is also a fully trained psychoanalyst. In so doing Tuckett places himself in a dauntingly demanding tradition of interdisciplinary research involving economics, sociology, and psychology so, when we read this book and become familiar with the theory of mind and thinking he has defined to explain the way financial markets work, we realize that he set the bar very high for himself, and has cleared it admirably.

The focus of this book is on understanding the financial crisis of 2008 although the study began in 2006. Thus, and fortuitously, I infer that this effort was not motivated by the self-conscious goal of preventing a future, catastrophic financial collapse. In a sense that gives the effort more credibility: it was not driven by what we clinicians might refer to as a grandiose rescue fantasy.

Tuckett's investigation is an effort to understand a human system in terms of the nuanced interplay of individual and collective conscious and unconscious motivations, and the conventions and rules governing and influencing the group behaviors of the community of financial professionals.

186

[[RunningHead]]

The APF Annual 2010: Langues et Courants Sexuels [Languages and Sexual Currents]1 edited by André Beetschen PUF, Paris, 2010; 224 pp; €24.50

Bertrand Colin

Bertrand Colin

66 avenue des Gobelins, 75013 Paris, France
E-mail: bcolin002@gmail.com

Bertrand Colin  

The APF Annual 2010 has just brought out its fourth volume, entitled Langues et courants sexuels [Languages and Sexual Currents]. Following its established procedure, it therefore cuts across several concepts. The most obvious is the concept of the ‘current’, evoking the Freudian duality between the tender and the sensual currents. Less obvious is the concept of ‘languages’, which has a nice ambiguity in the French title: does ‘sexual’ qualify ‘languages’ as well as ‘currents’? This question is loaded with a polemic that is readily fostered by the French alone on the international psychoanalytic scene: can the ‘sexual’ still claim its hegemony in the vast field of psychoanalytic concepts? If so, how? If not, why not? But more important still, in this Annual's title, is the ‘plural’ that defines ‘currents’ and ‘languages’. Languages exist only in the plural. Currents exist only in the plural. No language actually exists except in relation to other languages. This is a primary, insurmountable fact: entering into one language involves a self-differentiation from other languages and losing some of the possibilities among everything that is possible for the child. This probably also applies to the ‘sexual’ but in a more complex, more entangled way, never directly or purely. The status of the object changes when mastery, in anality, prevails over incorporation in orality. Tenderness, both a product and source of repression, leads to many different practices of sensuality - ranging from the most detrimental and destructive to the most promising and creative in love and hatred … Orality, anality, tenderness, sensuality … certainly have something to do with languages, beyond metaphor, in the specific sense that they are subject to the same principle of plurality and translatability as languages. Languages and sexual currents therefore have something in common here.

The papers collected in this Annual also show a plurality. They are linked, however, by an intuition. The multilingual experience reveals and amplifies a reality that is intrinsic to any analytic experience: using one language, moving from one to another and giving preference to a second over a first indicate resistances that it is helpful to identify, if only to respect them for as long as proves necessary. Resistances must be understood here in relation to the most archaic conflicts from which the ego tries to take shelter.

[[RunningHead]]

Exilium: Oltre la psicosi [Exile: Beyond Psychosis] by Marina Breccia3 Franco Angeli, Milano, 2011; 188 pp; €23.50

Francesco Conrotto

Francesco Conrotto

Via Aniello Falcone 13, I-80127 Napoli, Italia
E-mail: conrotto@mclink.it

Francesco Conrotto  

If, since the discovery of the unconscious, it is possible to claim that man is a stranger unto himself and that exile is his structural condition, in this book Marina Breccia demonstrates this with particular reference to the condition of psychosis. She also explores the concept in terms of the many ways it manifests itself in various aspects of our lives. Breccia draws on a wide range of sources, including art and literature, to enrich her reflections on the concept of exile.

Her starting point is that exile represents a leak in the mode of self-preservation, as defined by Freud in his early topographical model of the psyche. However, according to the author, the condition of exile should also be regarded as an extreme attempt by the ego to protect itself from total fragmentation by abolishing internal perception, which in turn entails a negation of the meaning of internal experience. This also leads to the withdrawal of the ego both from the external world and from the internal world.

The author sees the biblical myth of the expulsion of Adam and Eve from their earthly paradise as a model in which the human condition is understood in terms of exile. In her view, it is the sudden eruption of the drives into psychic life that forces the human into exile from his existence in the mode of pure self-preservation. But, once the instinctual has forced its way into psychic life, displacing equilibrium in favour of the death drive, the condition of exile also serves to protect the ego from an “excess of death” (p. 37).

And this is where the issue of a possible return from exile arises.

3Translated by Harriet Cooper.854

responseSet.title: On Psychic Elements in a Case of Autistoid Perversion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Psychic Elements in a Case of Autistoid Perversion

Bernd Nissen

Bernd Nissen

Limastr. 9a, 14163 Berlin, Germany - Bernd.Nissen@gmx.de

Bernd Nissen  

metapsychology, epistemology, mental quality, realization, pre-conception, transformation in O, paradox, autistoid perversion, presence

(Final version accepted 18 July 2012)

The paper explores the formation of psychic elements from an epistemological point of view, drawing on the work of Bion to examine a clinical case of autistoid perversion. Distinguishing the qualification of psychic elements from the realization of pre-conceptions, the paper argues that psychical elements are constituted through a mutually shared experience of presence, and so they should be understood in a paradoxical way - through being-O and transformations into K. These ideas are explored via a clinical case concerning a patient with an autistoid-perverse organization. The patient had been denied any bodily contact with her parents during her first year of life due to an infection; in later life she exhibited an autistoid coprophilic perversion. During the course of her treatment, as it became possible to break down the autistoid organization, the nameless contents surfaced in a mutually shared experience of presence. The analyst was able to hold on to their meaning, which was unavailable to the patient. The absent analyst, however, turned into the mother who ‘put the child down’ and was experienced by the patient as a suicidal threat. In being-O, the analyst was able to endure the paradox of being the one who ‘put her down’ in order not to put her down; the paradox of being-O functioned as a container for the destructive objectal dimension of the state of ‘being put down’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Jean Laplanche: 21 June 1924-6 May 2012

Patrick Merot

Patrick Merot

13 avenue Charles V, Nogent sur Marne, 94130, France
E-mail: pmerot@yahoo.fr

Patrick Merot  

Jean Laplanche died on 6 May 2012. The day of his death was the day of Freud's birth as if this were a sign of destiny recalling that he devoted his entire life as a psychoanalyst to the works of the inventor of psychoanalysis.

The importance of Jean Laplanche's contribution in the field of psychoanalysis has set its mark on several fields:

That of the study of the work of Freud, and the construction of his work proper: as a theorist he initially concentrated on the critical commentary of Freud and then developed his own original work focusing on what he called the generalized theory of seduction.

That of the translation in French of the complete works of Freud. In the French publishing landscape marked by an efflorescence of translations, undoubtedly very rich, but partial and disparate, he organized the translation of the complete works with extreme rigour and great determination. In France this translation is now a reference which is impossible to ignore. It had all begun with The Vocabulary of Psychoanalysis published by Jean Laplanche together with J.-B. Pontalis in 1968.

That of the psychoanalytical institution itself since he held a determining role in the foundation of the Psychoanalytical Association of France and in the definition of what was later to be called the ‘French model’.

567

responseSet.title: ‘No Entry’, an Invitation to Intrude, or Both? Reflections on a Group of Anorexic Patients
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘No Entry’, an Invitation to Intrude, or Both? Reflections on a Group of Anorexic Patients

Julian Stern

Julian Stern

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA, UK - julianstern@hotmail.com

Julian Stern  

applied analysis, transference, countertransference, dreams, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, intrusion, projective processes, anorexia

(Accepted for publication 1 November 2012)

This paper describes the twice-weekly psychoanalytic psychotherapy of a young woman who had undergone major bowel surgery in her early 20s, with no clear medical indication for the surgery. Whilst the concept of ‘No Entry’ described by Williams (1997a, b) aptly describes many features of more ‘typical’ anorexic patients, this paper describes a particular group of anorexic patients, referred by their physicians for multiple medical procedures; and proposes there is a group of anorexic patients, repeatedly referred for medical investigations, into whom particular types of entries occur. These are entries into the body ‘legitimized’ as medical, with a trajectory towards multiple procedures, examinations and surgical operations. Other entries (outside the medical setting) may occur in a state of altered consciousness, under the influence of alcohol or drugs, such that any wish for intrusion is disowned and denied. In both sets of events, intrusion is both invited, and consciously denied.

The case example illuminates some of these features, and aspects of the countertransference are also described. Attention is drawn to relevant research focusing on surgical intrusion. Finally, there is an exploration as to how such patients may invite intrusions into the body through surgery and medical procedures.

L'inconscient freudien. Recherche, écoute, métapsychologie [The Freudian Unconscious: Research, Listening and Metapsychology] edited by Gérard Bayle, Elisabeth Dahan-Soussan, Félicie Nayrou1PUF, Paris, 2010, Monographie et Débats de psychanalyse; 235 pp; €20

Claire Maurice

Claire Maurice

E-mail: clrmaurice@gmail.com

Claire Maurice  

The Freudian unconscious is a particularly topical subject now that a concept of the unconscious is so much used in many disciplines that it is in danger of being diluted into a catch-all term undermining the specific nature of a concept still alive and well in contemporary psychoanalysis. This was certainly the theme of the conference organized in November 2008 by the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, known as the President's Conference, from which all the papers have been collected in this extensive and important monograph. The first point to mention here is the rich variety of subjects addressed, listed under different chapters, making this book a comprehensive overview of Freud's concept of the unconscious. However, it is also the quality of each paper that makes this monograph a key reference work on the modernity of this concept.

The book is made easily accessible by the division into clearly differentiated themes that immediately open up the problematic of exactly what makes it specific from the perspective of other disciplines. A concept of the unconscious does not belong solely to the psychoanalytic domain but in fact covers several epistemological fields ranging from philosophy to current cognitive neurosciences. The papers by various authors therefore emphasize the complexity of this concept by demonstrating its evolution within Freud's thought itself. It is also important to indicate here the strong interest taken by some psychoanalysts in Freud's metapsychological revisions between 1914 and 1923, which introduced the problematic of narcissism, the definition of the second topography (ego, id, superego) and the new drive dualism with the life drive and the death drive, because these involve ‘an unconscious beyond sexual repression’ that overturns the early definitions. The notion of an ego that has partly become unconscious in terms of its inherent resistances brings about a topography of the unconscious that demarcates more uncertain areas requiring more complex consideration, as with contemporary clinical practice in relation to the act and the somatization.

responseSet.title: The Totalitarian Mind
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Totalitarian Mind99

Susana Fischbein

Susana Fischbein

Susana Fischbein  

The chair opened the panel with data related to the history of the two current German psychoanalytic societies. As a member of the German Psychoanalytic Society (DPG), Ursula Kreuzer-Haustein referred to the splitting between her Society, which joined the IPA in 2009, and the German Psychoanalytic Association (DPV) founded in 1950 by members of the traditional society who had left it after the war. The newly founded DPV was meant to be the ‘good’ society, and was immediately accepted by the IPA in 1951, whereas for several decades the DPG was considered the ‘bad’ Nazi society. Those years of hostile relationships were gradually followed by better contact and psychoanalytic exchanges between them. Kreuzer-Haustein considered this splitting as involving a powerful rejection of becoming aware of the historical responsibility of many members of the DPG, who, yielding to the Nazi racial laws, had first ousted their president, Max Eitingon, in 1933 and in 1935 had asked all the Jewish members to leave the DPG.

In The Totalitarian Unconscious, Michael Rustin primarily considers the systems of Nazism and Stalinism as the central examples of totalitarian systems. Nazism was responsible for the exiling of psychoanalysis from its German-speaking heartland, and this meant that the largest development of psychoanalysis in the period since the 1930s took place in the English-speaking world, in France and in Latin America.

What distinguishes totalitarianism from other kinds of authoritarian government is the dynamic role of a collective unconscious fantasy (essentially paranoid-schizoid) in the motivation and organization of the totalitarian system. These systems are dominated by extreme idealization, denigration and intolerance of reality. Large populations are prone to succumb to these states of mind as the outcome of extreme social disorganization and accompanying anxieties. Under these circumstances, individual leaders or groups of leaders emerge and achieve power by becoming, via introjective and projective processes, the recipients and amplifiers of anxieties and hatreds among traumatized populations. Rustin illustrated this situation through the defeats of Germany and Russia in World War I, a situation aggravated in Russia by the 1917 Revolution and the subsequent civil war.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Watch This Space! Research Studies in Depression: Three Studies Combining Clinical Psychoanalytical Research with Studies of Outcome2020

David Taylor

David Taylor

David Taylor  

The strength of psychoanalysis as a form of research and as a therapy derives from its detailed, long-term work with individuals; small numbers of self-selected individuals are seen over the course of any analytic

20Moderator: Heinz Böker (Germany). Panellists: Ulrich Barhke (Germany), Laura Bohleber (Switzerland & Germany), Holger Himmighofen v(Switzerland), David Taylor (UK), Heinz Weiss (Germany).1203

lifetime. In spite of its strengths, these features make psychoanalysis ill suited to test therapeutic propositions across samples or populations. But increasingly those who make the decisions about what to provide in publicly-funded or insurance-funded health services require evidence about a treatment's effectiveness, and as well that evidence should take a specific form: it must come from controlled trials in which samples or populations of subjects representative of the whole have been randomly allocated to different treatment conditions. This requirement has been a serious challenge for those who wish to maintain levels of public investment in the provision of psychoanalytic forms of therapy and its presence and status in academic stages of training and research.

In response, and without too much fanfare, several European centres have been engaged in the costly, time-consuming and challenging task of research into the outcome of medium or longer-term psychoanalytic treatments of patients suffering from depression. Why depression? Well, as Ulrich Bahrke and Johannes Kaufhold of Frankfurt, the first presenters to this Panel said, depression is up there amongst the most significant causes of human illness and disability.

None of the studies presented at the Panel are yet in a position to publish their outcome findings. But over the next two or three years each will do so. A substantial body of findings about patients with depression and the value of psychoanalytic treatments will then enter the public sphere. Whatever the direction of these findings, positive or negative, in terms of their scientific quality they will be equal, or in important respects superior, to any derived from studies of the outcome of other treatment approaches, such as antidepressant medications or cognitive-behavioural approaches. Moreover, those responsible for the three major studies presented at this Panel have been determined to stick by the values, principles and practice of psychoanalysis. They have been supported in this by the willingness of psychoanalysts and empirical researchers to join together in an effort both to remain commensurate with the true complexities of their subject matter, and to be adequate in terms of generally accepted standards for studies of the effectiveness of therapeutic interventions in groups or populations of patients.

responseSet.title: The Financial System and the Current Economic Crisis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Financial System and the Current Economic Crisis1919

Stephen Sonnenberg

Stephen Sonnenberg

Stephen Sonnenberg  

Holger Himmighofen introduced the panelists. David Tuckett began the discussion, stating that the financial markets froze in mid-2007, creating the financial crisis. He believes this crisis reflects a global loss of trust by investors, who now doubt that they will be paid back on their investments. He believes that markets are still edgy, he points out that credit is tight and liquidity minimal, and that there exist worthless assets and derivatives.

Tuckett offered background on how financial advisors think and work. They must construct conviction narratives in order to value assets. These narratives are fantastical. Tuckett asserted that psychoanalytic understanding can allow a neutral analysis of economics and finance, an analysis which is interdisciplinary in its approach. He believes such an investigation reveals that the markets have produced trauma and loss, and these reactions among investors and advisors need to be worked through, that there needs to be a widespread move towards a collective depressive position.

Tuckett spoke of his book, Minding The Markets: An Emotional Finance View of Financial Instability (2011), in which he describes interviews he conducted with successful finance managers before the 2007 crisis, and also spoke of his follow-up after the crisis. He found trends within the group: these managers experience uncertainty about their investment decisions, and must create pictures of the future that allow them to deal with the stress of uncertainty. Managers experience aversion and opportunity as they make decisions, as they deal with assets which are only paper, volatile and without intrinsic value.

Tuckett elaborated on the fantastical narratives: they are narratives about objects; the asset takes on the characteristics of an object. The asset manager has a conviction about an asset; the conviction is transformed into a fantasy about the asset because fundamentally the manager cannot know how the asset will perform. In fact, taking into account fees, 70% of managers perform below the overall market index, so to work with any confidence as a manager requires such a fantasy. Also, investing with managers requires a co-operative public, which also embraces the fantastical object narrative. Otherwise, the public would not engage in such an investment practice.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Resistance in Therapy and War: Psychoanalysis before and during the Nazi Occupation of Norway 1933-45

Håvard Nilsen

Håvard Nilsen

Res Publica, Box 9233, N-0134 Oslo, Norway - havard.nilsen@respublica.no

Håvard Nilsen  

On 28 March 1941, a year after the occupation of Norway, Vidkun Quisling of the Norwegian National Socialist Party (NS) gave a speech in Frankfurt on the alleged moral and scientific decay that had prevailed in Norway before the German occupation: “Psychoanalysis, invented by Freud, the Jew, had a great and harmful influence”, the newly installed ‘Minister President’ noted (Quisling, 1943, p. 8). According to Quisling, the country had been “enmeshed in the Jewish spirit”, through German emigre Marxists, British-American liberalists with anti-German sentiments as well as “sexual-pornograhic psychoanalysis”, imported through Wilhelm Reich. Reich, “a Jewish pornographer and quasi-scientist”, in Quisling's words, had “everywhere been greeted as a man with new and fruitful ideas.” He had “ruled” at the University, and his “destructive business among working class youth” had been hailed by “the ‘liberated’ decadent ‘intellectuals’ and protected by the Government Party (Labour)”, in Quisling's words (Quisling, 1943).

This speech shows the use of psychoanalysis as a symbol in Norwegian Nazi rhetoric, both before and during the occupation, indicating the strong currency of psychoanalysis as intellectual capital in Norwegian intellectual discourse, as well as a political point of controversy, epitomized by the presence of Wilhelm Reich during the years 1934-39. Because psychoanalysis was championed by Professor Harald Schjelderup (1895-1974) at the University of Oslo, Norway was perhaps the one country where psychoanalysis truly gained academic acceptance at an early stage and was largely regarded as a proper science; yet the debate surrounding it was highly politicized, more so than in many other countries.

Psychoanalysis in Norway before the Nazi occupation was partly a branch of academic psychology, partly a much-debated intellectual and political discourse. Harald Schjelderup had two professional aims related to

1Håvard Friis Nilsen (1969) is a historian and former Research Fellow at the University of Oslo 1999-2005. He was Visiting Scholar at the University of Cambridge from 2000-01 and is today Managing Director of the think tank and publishing house, Res Publica, Oslo, Norway. The present article is an English version of the article Havard Friis Nilsen (2010): “Widerstand in der Therapie und im Krieg 1933-1945. Die Psychoanalyse vor und wahrend der Besatzung Norwegens durch die Nationalsozialisten.” in Mitchell G. Ash (2010): Psychoanalyse unter Autoritaren und Totalitaren Regimen, Brandes & Apsel verlag, Vienna.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Memory and Desire, Tension and Conflict in ‘Late’ Bion55

Leigh Tobias

Leigh Tobias

Leigh Tobias  

Antonino Ferro (Italy) presided over this double panel made up of four analysts: Joseph Aguayo (USA), Lawrence Brown (USA), Barnet Malin (USA), and Rudi Vermote (Belgium), and two discussants, Robert Hinshelwood (UK) and Ferro himself. Hinshelwood served as discussant for Aguayo and Malin's papers, both of which focused on Bion's later period of work, in Los Angeles.

Hinshelwood noted that this was an historical moment due to the book just released by Aguayo and Malin (2013), Wilfred Bion: Los Angeles Seminars and Supervision. These taped seminars and supervision, given to members of the Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Institute in 1967, reflect the development of Bion's ideas from the ‘middle’ to ‘late’ period of theory building, the transition from ‘K’ to ‘O’. Hinshelwood noted Bion's use of intuition, the concept of ‘O’, and what that might mean. Most important here was being able to hear audio recordings of Bion's remarkably plain and direct English.

Aguayo posed the question of whether Bion was developing a divergence or an evolution of Kleinian technique, a focus of controversy for many years. Aguayo thought there was a theoretical caesura in Bion's analytic stance. Bion incorporated Klein's technique, then transformed it, defining new work to be done beyond the borders of current practices. Bion advocated that the analyst allow for a ‘penumbra’ of associations, loose definitions, urging analysts to develop their capacity for a disciplined sort of fresh listening receptivity in an unsaturated field.

Malin's paper included several taped clips of Bion's supervision of a group of young analysts. Malin highlighted the deliberate structural incompatibility between ‘K’ and ‘O’ regarding language and non-language based mental states. The audio tapes show Bion's efforts to demonstrate that the analytic stance of attending first to transformations in O (rather than those in K) profoundly alters the nature of psychoanalytic attention and interpretation. Bion worked hard to draw the students' attention away from abstract theories as they listened to the presented material.

[[RunningHead]]

What Does the Presentation of Case Material Tell Us about What Actually Happened in an Analysis and How Does It Do This?

Dale Boesky

Dale Boesky

614 Watkins, Birmingham, MI 48009, USA - dboesky@comcast.net

Dale Boesky  

Our Neglect of Context in the Inference of Unconscious Meaning

The above title suggested for this discussion (Blass, 2013, p. 1129) alludes to topics of such magnitude and complexity that in the present context I will primarily consider only one of these that has been perhaps most neglected - the process of contextualization that clinical presentations should make apparent, but often fail to. Theoretic pluralism has not eliminated ‘the’ unconscious as a theoretic bedrock source for the suffering of our patients. We have always shared the assumption that this suffering seeps into meaning-altered derivatives, which can only be accessed via an assumption that the links between manifest and latent meaning are disguised. But there is an abiding confusion about our methodology of inferring latent meaning from manifest content. Here, a paradoxical gap between theory and practice is nowhere more apparent or consequential. Our various alternative theoretic models provide diverse explanations for the causes of the transformation of unconscious affective pressures into their manifest derivative. But, without exception, these different theoretical models are, to varying degrees, silent or vague about the best methods of translating latent content to manifest content, and they always have been. Just as we have always known that there can be no meaning without context we have just as often ignored the task of stating why we have claimed that certain data warranted contextual significance. If we pin everything on context, why have we given so little attention to the methodology of contextualization?

To contextualize is to privilege and justify certain information. I use the word ‘justify’ here not in the connotation of legitimizing, let alone ‘validating’, but in the sense only of epistemic justification.1 It should be incumbent on our authors to show why they have decided to grant contextualizing importance to certain information. This is not at all the same as proving that this will validate the truth of the meaning justified by this claim.

[[RunningHead]]

What Does the Presentation of Case Material Tell Us about What Actually Happened in an Analysis and How Does It Do This?

Elias da Rocha Barros

Elias da Rocha Barros

Rua Tupi 579 apto 161, São Paulo 01233001, Brazil - erbarro@terra.com.br

Elias da Rocha Barros  

My first association to the topic of the nature and value of analytic case material (Blass, 2013, p. 1129-34) was that it should be discussed from two distinct approaches: (a) the value of analytic case material in illustrating a point of view or understanding, especially when conveyed as a lively experience for both author and reader of the paper; and (b) the value of the clinical material as evidence to confirm or reject new hypotheses or psychoanalytical theories - for the readers of a journal and for the scientific community of psychoanalysts.

It must be stated that my comments are those of an analyst who often reads and writes papers published in psychoanalytic journals and not of an epistemologist dealing with the complex matter of what can be considered scientific evidence acceptable by the community of psychoanalysts. (For an in-depth epistemologist approach, I suggest the recent book by Jorge Ahumada (2011), Insight: Essays on Psychoanalytic Knowing).

On beginning my reflection on the subject, it spontaneously occurred to me that the analyst-author writing a paper faces a paradox - one already pointed out by André Green (1992). He wants to communicate and convince, think critically and be right, all at the same time. This thought relates to the first approach, that of the value of the case material as illustrative. Then I recalled something which Jorge Ahumada had said with much humour in a presentation at the British Society: “The trouble with evidences is that they are not evident”, followed by another assertion of his, that this is the “reason why psychoanalysis needs to stick to the method” (Ahumada, 2011, p. 17). Ahumada was emphasizing that the search for evidence requires hard methodological work, is not self-evident and is dependent on the psychoanalytic method itself. This concerns the problem of evidence - the second issue under consideration in the study of the value of case material.

The Value of Analytic Case Material in Illustrating a Point of View or Understanding

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Black Client, White Therapist: Working with Race in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in South Africa

Zelda Knight

Zelda Knight

Johannesburg, South Africa - zknight@uj.ac.za

Zelda Knight  

race, black client, white client, psychoanalytic, psychotherapy, South Africa

(Accepted for publication 20 November 2012)

In post-apartheid South Africa we speak about race extensively. It permeates our workplace, weaves a thread through the fabric of our professional and personal lives, as well as our private conversations and public interactions with others. From within psychoanalytic theory, the thread weaves through the unknown content of our racialized unconscious. When there is a focus on race in the South African psychoanalytic context it largely takes the form of the struggle to articulate the complexities of working with difference, as Swartz notes, or the struggle to map out issues of race. Such struggles are not localized in South Africa, but strongly reflect a much broader struggle within the global psychoanalytic community, as mirrored in the expanding focus on race. Although the consulting rooms seem far removed from the ongoing political tensions that have recently emerged in South Africa, psychoanalytic psychotherapy remains a space of meaningful engagement with the other, and where the therapeutic dyad is one of racial difference it permits an encounter with our racialized unconscious. This article seeks to document the experience of my black client and my white response to her racial pain and struggle; in doing so, I describe the racial ‘contact’ between us and within us that triggers a racialized transference and countertransference dynamic, which contains the space for racial healing for both of us.

[[RunningHead]]

Oedipus the king: Quest for Self-Knowledge - Denial of Reality. Sophocles' Vision of Man and Psychoanalytic Concept Formation

Anders Zachrisson

Anders Zachrisson

Post-Box 1094, Blindern, N-0317
Oslo, Norway - johnaz@online.no;anders.zachrisson@psykologi.uio.no

Anders Zachrisson  

Oedipus, Sophocles, self-knowledge, a blind eye, conflict over knowledge, irony, history of psychoanalysis

(Final version accepted 30 April 2012)

In the Oedipus myth we find a dramatic representation of the child's passionate ties to its parents. In the play Oedipus the King, Sophocles relates the theme of the myth to the question of self-knowledge. This was the predominant reading in German 19th century thinking, and even as a student Freud was fascinated by Oedipus' character - not primarily as the protagonist of an oedipal drama, but as the solver of divine riddles and as an individual striving for self-knowledge. Inspired by Vellacott, Steiner has proposed an alternative reading of Oedipus the King as a play about a cover-up of the truth. The text supports both these arguments. The pivotal theme of the tragedy is Oedipus' conflict between his desire to know himself and his opposing wish to cover up the truth that will bring disaster. It is this complex character of Oedipus and the intensity of his conflict-ridden struggle for self-knowledge that has made the tragedy to a rich source of inspiration for psychoanalytic concept formation and understanding both of emotional and cognitive development up to our own time.

responseSet.title: The Wish for Annihilation in ‘Love-Death’ as Collapse of the Need for Recognition, in Wagner's Tristan und Isolde
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Wish for Annihilation in ‘Love-Death’ as Collapse of the Need for Recognition, in Wagner's Tristan und Isolde

Moshe Bergstein

Moshe Bergstein

4 Hakotzer St., Ramat-Hasharon, 47411, Israel - moshebergstein@gmail.com

Moshe Bergstein  

Tristan, Isolde, Wagner, Liebestod, recognition, opera

(Accepted for publication 27 November 2012)

Wagner's Tristan und Isolde holds a central position in Western music and culture. It is shown to demonstrate consequences of interruption of developmental processes involving the need for recognition of subjectivity, resulting in the collapse of this need into the wish for annihilation of self and other through ‘love-death’ [Liebestod]. A close reading of the musical language of the opera reveals how this interruption is demonstrated, and the consequent location of identity outside of language, particularly suitable for expression in music. Isolde's dynamics are presented as distinct from that of Tristan, and in contrast to other interpretations of Tristan and Isolde's love as an attack on the Oedipal order, or as a regressive wish for pre-Oedipal union. Isolde's Act I narrative locates the origin of her desire in the protagonists' mutual gaze at a traumatic moment. In this moment powerful and contrasting emotions converge, evoking thwarted developmental needs, and arousing the fantasy of redemption in love-death. By removing the magical elements, Wagner enables a deeper understanding of the characters' positions in relation to each other, each with his or her own needs for recognition and traumatic experiences. These positions invite mutual identifications resulting in rising tension between affirmation of identity and annihilation, with actual death as the only possible psychic solution. The dynamics described in the opera demonstrate the function of music and opera in conveying meaning which is not verbally expressible.

Psychoanalytic Diagnosis; by Nancy McWilliams; Guilford Press, New York, 2011, second edition; 426 pp; $60.00

Otto Kernberg MD

Otto Kernberg

21 Bloomingdale Road, White Plains
NY 10605, USA
E-mail: okernber@med.cornell.edu

Otto Kernberg  

This new, significantly enriched and updated edition of Nancy McWilliams's classic text is an important contribution to psychoanalytic literature. It outlines the application of psychoanalytic findings to character formation and personality disorders, the diagnostic evaluation of personality structure and its implications for treatment indications, and the therapeutic implications of the defensive organization of different personality disorders. It is essential reading for psychoanalytic psychotherapists, clinical psychologists, psychiatrists and mental health professionals in a broad sense, and, for the psychoanalyst, it provides an impressive, updated history of the crucial contributions that psychoanalysis has been making in all these fields.

Part One of the book is structured in terms of the underlying theoretical assumptions and main psychoanalytic findings that underlie the proposed description of the types of character organization that follow in the second part. These conceptual issues include an excellent discussion of why diagnosis is important in psychoanalytic treatment and psychoanalytic-oriented psychotherapies, and the general theoretic psychoanalytic orientations that influence the diagnosis of character. There are several chapters dealing with psychological development in terms of the severity of personality disorders, and the constellations of primary and secondary defensive processes that correspond, respectively, to the most severe and less severe personality disorders.

responseSet.title: Major Panel: Transformations of Emotional Experience
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Major Panel: Transformations of Emotional Experience11

Josh Cohen

Josh Cohen

Josh Cohen  

This IPA Major Panel brought together two discussants from very different psychoanalytic cultures to address the questions raised by Lia Pistiner de Cortiñas' (Asociación Psicoanalítica de Buenos Aires) paper Transformations of emotional experience, each bringing intriguingly different perspectives to the same clinical material.

Pistiner's paper centred on her work with Ana, a young woman who appeared on first encounter to be profoundly dissociated from her own inner life and unable to give even rudimentary expression to her suffering. The history that emerged was a traumatic one. Ana's childhood was indelibly marked by the “disappearance” of her student brother by the brutal 1970s regime in Argentina, and its devastating impact on her family, now reduced to her emotionally “deaf” mother and army officer father.

Pistiner sought to conceptualize this material using the Bionian framework of “transformation”, which she amplified with ideas from Donald Meltzer and Susanne Langer. When Ana first arrived, she suggests, her inner life was a disorganized mass of beta elements variously expressed in confusion, physical exhaustion and a near absence of observable affect, a sense of psychic deadness countertransferentially registered in the analyst's struggle with her frequent lack of interest in the patient. She ascribes this to the desperately inadequate parental functions that have left Ana with a very weak capacity to represent her experience to herself. The analyst of such a patient is left, Pistiner points out, “with a lack of instruments … to solve problems”, that is, without the benefit of the mental apparatus that would transform raw emotional experience into thinkable thoughts.

The analysis is thus presented as the narrative of the slow and faltering development of such an apparatus, and of the gradual growth of Ana's symbolic function and capacity to suffer. This is dramatized most strikingly in the account of a somatic disturbance during the first year of analysis, in the form of the “eruption” of the patient's hand in a severe inflammation which cracked her skin open, symptoms which a series of medical consultations failed to explain. These are associated by Ana to the lack of membrane or “curtain” between her child self and her parents' sexuality. These, patient and analyst conjecture, produced a flood of uncontained excitement and fear which compulsive masturbation emphatically failed to contain. The link enables the raw physical suffering of the hand to be transformed into a symbolic repository of emotional pain.
